[DOB-1219] - ED to Hosp-Admission (Discharged) in NH PAV 2 Giles (continued)
ED Provider Note (continued)
I have personally performed a face-to-face diagnostic evaluation of the patient. I have reviewed and agree with the
care plan. My findings are as follows:
HPI: Pt is a 69 yr/o female presenting to the ED for an MVC that occurred 3 days ago. Her main complaint is lower
abdominal pain. The pt was a restrained driver when a car ran into her while she was driving forward. She noticed
body aches the next night, which has progressively worsened. She has tried pain medications and heating pad. Associated symptom is dizziness at the time of the accident, and she continues to experience dizzy spells. The pt
denies being on blood thinners. She has a medical hx of DM and HTN. PE:
GEN: No acute distress
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally no wheezes
Heart: Regular rate and rhythm no murmur
Abdomen: Soft nontender nondistended
Extremities: No gross injury or deformity
EKG interpreted by me:
Time: 1013
Rate: 58
Sinus bradycardia, Poor R wave progression, Nonspecific ST changes. Progress: 
1153: I spoke with Dr. Boseman about the patient's imaging displaying appendicitis. Assessment/plan: Admit.
Impression: 
ICD-10-
CM
ICD-9-CM
1. Appendicitis, unspecified appendicitis type K37 541
2. Hypokalemia E87.6 276.8
3. Pancreatic lesion K86.9 577.9
Disposition: Admit. Merit-Based Incentive Payment System
Data Reporting Section
The following information is for internal use only for reporting specific care related data as required by The Medicare
Access & Chip Reauthorization Act of 2015. This data will be made available in aggregate to respective government
and regulatory agencies. CMS #317) Preventative Care and Screening: Screening for High Blood Pressure and Follow-Up Documented
* All patient's aged 18 and older who have been screened for high blood pressure and recommended follow-up. Did the patient have SBP > 120 or DBP > 80 while in the ED? Yes
For those with elevated blood pressure, did you recommend follow-up for recheck? No, patient was admitted

ED Provider Note (continued)
I have personally performed a face-to-face diagnostic evaluation of the patient. I have reviewed and agree with the
care plan. My findings are as follows:
HPI: Pt is a 69 yr/o female presenting to the ED for an MVC that occurred 3 days ago. Her main complaint is lower
abdominal pain. The pt was a restrained driver when a car ran into her while she was driving forward. She noticed
body aches the next night, which has progressively worsened. She has tried pain medications and heating pad.
Associated symptom is dizziness at the time of the accident, and she continues to experience dizzy spells. The pt
denies being on blood thinners. She has a medical hx of DM and HTN.
PE:
GEN: No acute distress
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally no wheezes
Heart: Regular rate and rhythm no murmur
Abdomen: Soft nontender nondistended
Extremities: No gross injury or deformity
EKG interpreted by me:
Time: 1013
Rate: 58
Sinus bradycardia, Poor R wave progression, Nonspecific ST changes.
Progress:
1153: I spoke with Dr. Boseman about the patient's imaging displaying appendicitis.
Assessment/plan: Admit.
Impression:
ICD-10- ICD-9-CM
CM
1. Appendicitis, unspecified appendicitis type K37 541
2. Hypokalemia E87.6 276.8
3. Pancreatic lesion K86.9 577.9
Disposition: Admit.
Merit-Based Incentive Payment System

and regulatory agencies.

[DOB-1215] - ED to Hosp-Admission (Discharged) in NH PAV 2 Giles (continued)
ED Provider Note (continued)
PCP is Center, Park Duvalle Community Health
HPI:
Patient is a 69 yr/o female with a history of diabetes, who presents to the emergency department complaining of lower
abdominal pain and lightheadedness.. She states that she was a restrained driver involved in MVA. Her vehicle was
struck on the passenger side by a large truck, reports positive airbag deployment, denies LOC or head injury. She
states that she was seen and evaluated at Baptist East and then discharged home. She states since the event she
has been experiencing lower abdominal pain and feeling lightheaded. She stated "I think they did a full body scan ".
After reviewing the electronic medical records it appeared the patient had a CT head and CT C-spine and no further
blood work or imaging. Additionally, she reports an intermittent left-sided chest pain described as sharp nonradiating
pain that started as soon as I walked into the room, she states it only lasts a few seconds and that resolved and
reports no associating symptoms with the chest pain. She also reports experiencing lightheadedness that has been
occurring since accident, she states after the accident she felt like her head was thrown around. Pt denies headache,
numbness/tingling or weakness, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, dysuria or hematuria, or any other
associating symptoms. . Pt denies tobacco, alcohol or drug use. Review Medical Records:
Reviewed in Epic including triage summary, medications, PMH, allergies and chart review. Past Medical History:
Diagnosis Date
• Diabetes mellitus (CMS/HCC) • Hypertension

Ct Abdomen & Pelvis W Iv Contrast Without Oral Contrast
Result Date: 9/6/2021
REVIEWING YOUR TEST RESULTS IN MYNORTONCHART IS NOT A SUBSTITUTE FOR DISCUSSING THOSE
RESULTS WITH YOUR HEALTH CARE PROVIDER. PLEASE CONTACT YOUR PROVIDER VIA
MYNORTONCHART TO DISCUSS ANY QUESTIONS OR CONCERNS YOU MAY HAVE REGARDING THESE
TEST RESULTS. RADIOLOGY REPORT FACILITY: NORTON HOSPITAL UNIT/AGE/GENDER: N.ED ER
AGE:69 Y SEX:F PATIENT NAME/DOB: LANG, BRENDA, D [DOB-1315] UNIT NUMBER: EP00142214
ACCOUNT NUMBER: 10115286932 ACCESSION NUMBER: EPN21CT757905 CT of the abdomen and pelvis with
contrast dated September 6, 2021. INDICATION: abd pain. Motor vehicle crash. Lower abdominal pain. TECHNIQUE:
Routine IV contrasted CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed. Dose reduction techniques were employed per
ALARA protocol. COMPARISON: Correlation is made with a CT lung screening exam dated May 14, 2021 FINDINGS:
Motion artifact degrades image quality, especially at the lung bases and upper abdomen. There may be fatty infiltration
of the liver. Gallbladder, spleen and adrenal glands are grossly unremarkable given motion. Cystic lesion at the
anterior neck of the pancreas measures 1.3 cm and likely has a thin septation. Multiseptated cystic lesion at the
inferior anterior head of the pancreas measures 2 cm. Pancreatic duct in the medial body, head and neck is upper
limits of normal. No biliary duct dilation. At least partial duplication of the right renal collecting system. Kidneys are
malrotated with the renal hilar regions facing anteriorly. Urinary bladder is unremarkable. Uterus remains. Prominent
vessels flank the uterus, left greater than right which can be seen with pelvic congestion syndrome. Stomach is not
well-distended. Appendix is dilated with wall thickening and periappendiceal inflammatory change. Appendix measures
1 cm in diameter. No bowel obstruction. No free intraperitoneal air. No ascites or evidence of abscess. Atherosclerotic
vascular calcification. No lymphadenopathy. Sequelae from granulomatous disease within the chest. Left basilar
atelectasis/scarring. There are degenerative changes with no suspicious osseous lesion. No evidence of fracture and
for limitations from motion. IMPRESSION: 1. Acute appendicitis. No free intraperitoneal air or evidence of abscess. This finding was discussed with Santina Arnold in the ER 9/6/2021 at 11:31 AM. 2. Cystic pancreatic lesions may be
serous or mucinous. Comparison with prior imaging is recommended if available and attention on follow-up is

Radiology:
Ct Abdomen & Pelvis W Iv Contrast Without Oral Contrast
Result Date: 9/6/2021
REVIEWING YOUR TEST RESULTS IN MYNORTONCHART IS NOT A SUBSTITUTE FOR DISCUSSING THOSE
RESULTS WITH YOUR HEALTH CARE PROVIDER. PLEASE CONTACT YOUR PROVIDER VIA
MYNORTONCHART TO DISCUSS ANY QUESTIONS OR CONCERNS YOU MAY HAVE REGARDING THESE
TEST RESULTS. RADIOLOGY REPORT FACILITY: NORTON HOSPITAL UNIT/AGE/GENDER: N.ED ER
AGE:69 Y SEX:F PATIENT NAME/DOB: LANG, BRENDA, D [DOB-1315] UNIT NUMBER: EP00142214
ACCOUNT NUMBER: 10115286932 ACCESSION NUMBER: EPN21CT757905 CT of the abdomen and pelvis with
contrast dated September 6, 2021. INDICATION: abd pain. Motor vehicle crash. Lower abdominal pain. TECHNIQUE:
Routine IV contrasted CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed. Dose reduction techniques were employed per
ALARA protocol. COMPARISON: Correlation is made with a CT lung screening exam dated May 14, 2021 FINDINGS:
Motion artifact degrades image quality, especially at the lung bases and upper abdomen. There may be fatty infiltration
of the liver. Gallbladder, spleen and adrenal glands are grossly unremarkable given motion. Cystic lesion at the
anterior neck of the pancreas measures 1.3 cm and likely has a thin septation. Multiseptated cystic lesion at the
inferior anterior head of the pancreas measures 2 cm. Pancreatic duct in the medial body, head and neck is upper
limits of normal. No biliary duct dilation. At least partial duplication of the right renal collecting system. Kidneys are
malrotated with the renal hilar regions facing anteriorly. Urinary bladder is unremarkable. Uterus remains. Prominent
vessels flank the uterus, left greater than right which can be seen with pelvic congestion syndrome. Stomach is not
well-distended. Appendix is dilated with wall thickening and periappendiceal inflammatory change. Appendix measures
1 cm in diameter. No bowel obstruction. No free intraperitoneal air. No ascites or evidence of abscess. Atherosclerotic
vascular calcification. No lymphadenopathy. Sequelae from granulomatous disease within the chest. Left basilar
atelectasis/scarring. There are degenerative changes with no suspicious osseous lesion. No evidence of fracture and
for limitations from motion. IMPRESSION: 1. Acute appendicitis. No free intraperitoneal air or evidence of abscess.
This finding was discussed with Santina Arnold in the ER 9/6/2021 at 11:31 AM. 2. Cystic pancreatic lesions may be
serous or mucinous. Comparison with prior imaging is recommended if available and attention on follow-up is

after no acute process was seen. Pt then that's that the next night she developed generalized abdominal pain with no
associated emesis or nausea. Pt states she was unable to tolerate a diet from poor appetite. This morning, pt states

Discharge Summary Note (continued)
Discharge date and time: 9/7/2021
Admitting Physician: Matthew c Bozeman, MD
Discharge Physician: Matthew C Bozeman, MD
Admission Diagnoses: Appendicitis [K37]
Discharge Diagnoses: Appendicitis [K37]
Discharged Condition: good
Admission HPI:Brenda D Lang is a 69 yr/o female is a 69 y/o F with hx of HTN and DM who presents with abdominal
pain x2 days. Pt states that she was recently in an MVC x3 days ago, she was worked up in an OSH and sent home
after no acute process was seen. Pt then that's that the next night she developed generalized abdominal pain with no
associated emesis or nausea. Pt states she was unable to tolerate a diet from poor appetite. This morning, pt states
that her abdominal pain shifted the the RLQ and worsened which prompted her hospital visit.
WBC within normal limits, pt afebrile normotensive, HR within normal limits.
CT scan significant for acute appendicitis with no free air.
Pt states she does not take blood thinner medications, most recent meal was a few bites of apple sauce at 8am.
Procedures:
Procedure(s) (LRB):
LAPAROSCOPIC APPENDECTOMY (N/A)
Hospital Course: Brenda D Lang is a 69 yr/o female with hx of HTN and DM who presents for acute appendicitis.
The pt was brought to the OR on 9/6/2021 for the above procedure. SHe tolerated the procedure well, see dictated
operative note for detailed summary. Her diet was gradually advanced as GI function returned. At the time of
discharge she was tolerating a regular diet and having bowel movements. Also, at the time of discharge her pain was
controlled on oral medication and she was ambulating without assistance.
Consults:
IP CONSULT TO GENERAL SURGERY
Significant Diagnostic Studies: labs and radiology
Discharge Exam:
See daily note from date of dc
Disposition:
Home
Patient Instructions:
Current Discharge Medication List

ACCOUNT NUMBER: 10115286932 ACCESSION NUMBER: EPN21CT757905 CT of the abdomen and pelvis with
contrast dated September 6, 2021. INDICATION: abd pain. Motor vehicle crash. Lower abdominal pain. TECHNIQUE:
Routine IV contrasted CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed. Dose reduction techniques were employed per
ALARA protocol. COMPARISON: Correlation is made with a CT lung screening exam dated May 14, 2021 FINDINGS:
Motion artifact degrades image quality, especially at the lung bases and upper abdomen. There may be fatty infiltration
of the liver. Gallbladder, spleen and adrenal glands are grossly unremarkable given motion. Cystic lesion at the
anterior neck of the pancreas measures 1.3 cm and likely has a thin septation. Multiseptated cystic lesion at the
inferior anterior head of the pancreas measures 2 cm. Pancreatic duct in the medial body, head and neck is upper
limits of normal. No biliary duct dilation. At least partial duplication of the right renal collecting system. Kidneys are

malrotated with the renal hilar regions facing anteriorly. Urinary bladder is unremarkable. Uterus remains. Prominent

1 cm in diameter. No bowel obstruction. No free intraperitoneal air. No ascites or evidence of abscess. Atherosclerotic
vascular calcification. No lymphadenopathy. Sequelae from granulomatous disease within the chest. Left basilar
atelectasis/scarring. There are degenerative changes with no suspicious osseous lesion. No evidence of fracture and
for limitations from motion. IMPRESSION: 1. Acute appendicitis. No free intraperitoneal air or evidence of abscess. This finding was discussed with Santina Arnold in the ER 9/6/2021 at 11:31 AM. 2. Cystic pancreatic lesions may be

I personally have examined the patient and interviewed the patient with the resident staff on rounds today. I have
reviewed the history, data, problems, assessment and plan with the resident staff as well. 69 y/o lady s/p MVA a few days ago, went to baptist, had head CT and no evidence of intracranial abnormality, but
developed abdominal pain, RLQ in nature, Sharp stabbing, constant yesterday. Labs show a normal WBC, no anemia, no acidosis. CT abdomen personally reviewed, and does appear to be acute appendicitis with a fecalith present. Started on antibiotics. Risks, benefits, and alternatives discussed including bleeding, infection, possible need for open
procedure, and that she can't be treated with antibiotics alone given the fecalith present, and she wishes to proceed. Electronically signed by Bozeman, Matthew C, MD at 9/6/2021 9:44 PM

Patient Identification:

Sex: female
DOB: 9/1/1952
MRN: EP00142214 Date of admission: 9/6/2021 Chief Complaint: Abdominal pain
History of Present Illness: 
Brenda D Lang is a 69 yr/o female is a 69 y/o F with hx of HTN and DM who presents with abdominal pain x2 days. Pt
states that she was recently in an MVC x3 days ago, she was worked up in an OSH and sent home after not acute
process was seen. Pt then that's that the next night she developed generalized abdominal pain with no associated
emesis or nausea. Pt states she was unable to tolerate a diet from poor appetite. This morning, pt states that her
abdominal pain shifted the the RLQ and worsened which prompted her hospital visit. WBC within normal limits, pt afebrile normotensive, HR within normal limits. CT scan significant for acute appendicitis with no free air. Pt states she does not take blood thinner medications, most recent meal was a few bites of apple sauce at 8am. Problem List:

AGE:69 Y SEX:F PATIENT NAME/DOB: LANG, BRENDA, D [DOB-1315] UNIT NUMBER: EP00142214
ACCOUNT NUMBER: 10115286932 ACCESSION NUMBER: EPN21CT757905 CT of the abdomen and pelvis with
contrast dated September 6, 2021. INDICATION: abd pain. Motor vehicle crash. Lower abdominal pain. TECHNIQUE:
Routine IV contrasted CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed. Dose reduction techniques were employed per
ALARA protocol. COMPARISON: Correlation is made with a CT lung screening exam dated May 14, 2021 FINDINGS:
Motion artifact degrades image quality, especially at the lung bases and upper abdomen. There may be fatty infiltration
of the liver. Gallbladder, spleen and adrenal glands are grossly unremarkable given motion. Cystic lesion at the
anterior neck of the pancreas measures 1.3 cm and likely has a thin septation. Multiseptated cystic lesion at the
inferior anterior head of the pancreas measures 2 cm. Pancreatic duct in the medial body, head and neck is upper
limits of normal. No biliary duct dilation. At least partial duplication of the right renal collecting system. Kidneys are
malrotated with the renal hilar regions facing anteriorly. Urinary bladder is unremarkable. Uterus remains. Prominent
vessels flank the uterus, left greater than right which can be seen with pelvic congestion syndrome. Stomach is not
well-distended. Appendix is dilated with wall thickening and periappendiceal inflammatory change. Appendix measures
1 cm in diameter. No bowel obstruction. No free intraperitoneal air. No ascites or evidence of abscess. Atherosclerotic

[DOB-1012] - ED to Hosp-Admission (Discharged) in NH PAV 2 Giles (continued)
OP Notes (continued)
PATIENT NAME/DOB: LANG, BRENDA D [DOB-1315]
UNIT NUMBER: EP00142214
ACCOUNT NUMBER: 10115286932
DATE OF OPERATION(S)/PROCEDURE(S): [DOB-1011]
SURGEON: Matthew C Bozeman, MD
PROCEDURE PERFORMED: Laparoscopic appendectomy.
PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: Acute appendicitis.
POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: Acute appendicitis.
RESIDENT SURGEONS: Elizabeth Bruenderman and Brandon Ryvkin
ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: Less than 5 mL.
ANESTHESIA: General endotracheal. SPECIMENS: Appendix.
COMPLICATIONS: None immediate.
INDICATIONS FOR PROCEDURE: Ms. Lang is a very pleasant 69-year-old lady who was involved in a
motor vehicle accident 2 days ago. She underwent cranial imaging at Baptist. Then her abdominal pain
worsened. She underwent a CT scan at Norton's which revealed no evidence of intra-abdominal injuries, but signs of acute appendicitis on imaging. I saw her, explained the risks, benefits, and alternatives of
proceeding with appendectomy, including bleeding, infection, possible injury to surrounding structures, possible development of an abscess postoperatively, and the fact that she did have a fecalith, necessitating
appendectomy. She wished to proceed. DESCRIPTION OF PROCEDURE: After obtaining informed consent and discussing risks, benefits and
alternatives, Ms. Lang was taken to the operating room and laid supine on the OR table. General
endotracheal anesthesia was administered without incident. Preoperative antibiotics were given. Her
abdomen was sterilely prepped and draped in standard fashion. We then held a standard surgical timeout
and then infiltrated the skin below the umbilicus with 0.25% Marcaine with epinephrine, divided down
through the skin and subcutaneous tissues, grasped the fascia, elevated it and incised it with an 11-blade, and placed a Hasson trocar in standard fashion. We then insufflated her abdomen and placed 2 more
trocars, both of which were 5 mm in nature; 1 in the left lower quadrant, 1 in the lower midline. We visualized the appendix. It was inflamed at the mid portion and tip, but the base appeared to be
relatively spared of disease. We then dissected at the base of the appendix between the appendix and the
mesoappendix. Then we transected the mesoappendix using a Harmonic Scalpel and fired a purple load of

atelectasis/scarring. There are degenerative changes with no suspicious osseous lesion. No evidence of fracture and
for limitations from motion. IMPRESSION: 1. Acute appendicitis. No free intraperitoneal air or evidence of abscess.
This finding was discussed with Santina Arnold in the ER 9/6/2021 at 11:31 AM. 2. Cystic pancreatic lesions may be
serous or mucinous. Comparison with prior imaging is recommended if available and attention on follow-up is
recommended. 3. Atherosclerosis. Dictated by: Rebecca Feller, M.D. Images and Report reviewed and interpreted by:

OP Notes (continued)
the GIA stapler across the base of the appendix, taking care not to include any other structures. Once this
was done, the appendix was placed in a bag, brought out through the umbilical trocar incision. We
inspected for hemostasis, was excellent. There were no immediate complications. We then desufflated her
abdomen, removed her ports under direct visualization, closed the umbilical fascia with 0 Vicryl interrupted
sutures, 4-0 subcuticular sutures were applied to the skin. She tolerated the procedure well. There were no immediate complications. As the attending surgeon, I was present for the entirety of the procedure. At the end of the case, all sponge
and needle counts were correct x2 per OR staff. Matthew C Bozeman, MD

GI: 
Negative (-) for GERD. ROS Additional Findings: From Surg H&P:
69 y/o lady s/p MVA a few days ago, went to baptist, had head CT and no evidence of intracranial abnormality, but
developed abdominal pain, RLQ in nature, Sharp stabbing, constant yesterday. Labs show a normal WBC, no anemia, no acidosis. CT abdomen personally reviewed, and does appear to be acute appendicitis with a fecalith present. Started on antibiotics. Risks, benefits, and alternatives discussed including bleeding, infection, possible need for open
procedure, and that she can't be treated with antibiotics alone given the fecalith present, and she wishes to proceed. Physical Exam

Clinical Notes (continued)
REVIEWING YOUR TEST RESULTS IN MYNORTONCHART IS NOT A SUBSTITUTE FOR DISCUSSING THOSE
RESULTS WITH YOUR HEALTH CARE PROVIDER. PLEASE CONTACT YOUR PROVIDER VIA
MYNORTONCHART TO DISCUSS ANY QUESTIONS OR CONCERNS YOU MAY HAVE REGARDING THESE
TEST RESULTS. RADIOLOGY REPORT FACILITY: NORTON HOSPITAL UNIT/AGE/GENDER: N.ED ER
AGE:69 Y SEX:F PATIENT NAME/DOB: LANG, BRENDA, D [DOB-1315] UNIT NUMBER: EP00142214
ACCOUNT NUMBER: 10115286932 ACCESSION NUMBER: EPN21CT757905 CT of the abdomen and pelvis with
contrast dated September 6, 2021. INDICATION: abd pain. Motor vehicle crash. Lower abdominal pain. TECHNIQUE:
Routine IV contrasted CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed. Dose reduction techniques were employed per
ALARA protocol. COMPARISON: Correlation is made with a CT lung screening exam dated May 14, 2021 FINDINGS:
Motion artifact degrades image quality, especially at the lung bases and upper abdomen. There may be fatty infiltration
of the liver. Gallbladder, spleen and adrenal glands are grossly unremarkable given motion. Cystic lesion at the
anterior neck of the pancreas measures 1.3 cm and likely has a thin septation. Multiseptated cystic lesion at the
inferior anterior head of the pancreas measures 2 cm. Pancreatic duct in the medial body, head and neck is upper
limits of normal. No biliary duct dilation. At least partial duplication of the right renal collecting system. Kidneys are
malrotated with the renal hilar regions facing anteriorly. Urinary bladder is unremarkable. Uterus remains. Prominent
vessels flank the uterus, left greater than right which can be seen with pelvic congestion syndrome. Stomach is not
well-distended. Appendix is dilated with wall thickening and periappendiceal inflammatory change. Appendix measures
1 cm in diameter. No bowel obstruction. No free intraperitoneal air. No ascites or evidence of abscess. Atherosclerotic
vascular calcification. No lymphadenopathy. Sequelae from granulomatous disease within the chest. Left basilar
atelectasis/scarring. There are degenerative changes with no suspicious osseous lesion. No evidence of fracture and
for limitations from motion. IMPRESSION: 1. Acute appendicitis. No free intraperitoneal air or evidence of abscess. This finding was discussed with Santina Arnold in the ER 9/6/2021 at 11:31 AM. 2. Cystic pancreatic lesions may be
serous or mucinous. Comparison with prior imaging is recommended if available and attention on follow-up is
recommended. 3. Atherosclerosis. Dictated by: Rebecca Feller, M.D. Images and Report reviewed and interpreted by:
Rebecca Feller, M.D. <PS><Electronically signed by: Rebecca Feller, M.D.> [DOB-429] 1135 D: [DOB-886] 1127 T:
[DOB-885] 1127
Assessment:
Principal Problem:
Appendicitis (9/6/2021) POA: Unknown
1 Day Post-Op Procedure(s) (LRB):
LAPAROSCOPIC APPENDECTOMY (N/A)
Plan:
- K+ replacement - AROBF, CLD, non carbonated if positive flatus. - Void check, bladder scan if negative. - OOB, Ambulate
-D/C later in the day if return of bowel function, voiding, and benign abdominal exam. Leslie Fogwe, MD
9/7/2021

AGE:69 Y SEX:F PATIENT NAME/DOB: LANG, BRENDA, D [DOB-1315] UNIT NUMBER: EP00142214
ACCOUNT NUMBER: 10115286932 ACCESSION NUMBER: EPN21CT757905 CT of the abdomen and pelvis with
contrast dated September 6, 2021. INDICATION: abd pain. Motor vehicle crash. Lower abdominal pain. TECHNIQUE:
Routine IV contrasted CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed. Dose reduction techniques were employed per
ALARA protocol. COMPARISON: Correlation is made with a CT lung screening exam dated May 14, 2021 FINDINGS:
Motion artifact degrades image quality, especially at the lung bases and upper abdomen. There may be fatty infiltration
of the liver. Gallbladder, spleen and adrenal glands are grossly unremarkable given motion. Cystic lesion at the
anterior neck of the pancreas measures 1.3 cm and likely has a thin septation. Multiseptated cystic lesion at the
inferior anterior head of the pancreas measures 2 cm. Pancreatic duct in the medial body, head and neck is upper
limits of normal. No biliary duct dilation. At least partial duplication of the right renal collecting system. Kidneys are
malrotated with the renal hilar regions facing anteriorly. Urinary bladder is unremarkable. Uterus remains. Prominent
vessels flank the uterus, left greater than right which can be seen with pelvic congestion syndrome. Stomach is not
well-distended. Appendix is dilated with wall thickening and periappendiceal inflammatory change. Appendix measures
1 cm in diameter. No bowel obstruction. No free intraperitoneal air. No ascites or evidence of abscess. Atherosclerotic

CT of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast dated September 6, 2021.
INDICATION: abd pain. Motor vehicle crash. Lower abdominal pain. TECHNIQUE: Routine IV contrasted CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed. Dose reduction techniques were employed
per ALARA protocol. COMPARISON: Correlation is made with a CT lung screening exam dated May 14, 2021
FINDINGS: Motion artifact degrades image quality, especially at the lung bases and upper abdomen. There may be fatty infiltration of the liver. Gallbladder, spleen and adrenal glands are grossly unremarkable given motion. Cystic
lesion at the anterior neck of the pancreas measures 1.3 cm and likely has a thin septation. Multiseptated cystic lesion at
the inferior anterior head of the pancreas measures 2 cm. Pancreatic duct in the medial body, head and neck is upper limits of
normal. No biliary duct dilation. At least partial duplication of the right renal collecting system. Kidneys are malrotated with the renal hilar regions facing
anteriorly. Urinary bladder is unremarkable. Uterus remains. Prominent vessels flank the uterus, left greater than right which can be seen
with pelvic congestion syndrome. Stomach is not well-distended. Appendix is dilated with wall thickening and periappendiceal inflammatory change. Appendix
measures 1 cm in diameter. No bowel obstruction. No free intraperitoneal air. No ascites or evidence of abscess. Atherosclerotic vascular calcification. No lymphadenopathy. Sequelae from granulomatous disease within the chest. Left basilar atelectasis/scarring. There are degenerative changes with no suspicious osseous lesion. No evidence of fracture and for limitations from motion.
IMPRESSION:
1. Acute appendicitis. No free intraperitoneal air or evidence of abscess. This finding was discussed with Santina Arnold in the
ER 9/6/2021 at 11:31 AM. 2. Cystic pancreatic lesions may be serous or mucinous. Comparison with prior imaging is recommended if available and
attention on follow-up is recommended. 3. Atherosclerosis. Dictated by: Rebecca Feller, M.D.

Louisville KY 40202-1800 Acct #: 210384988
Adm: 9/6/2021, D/C: 9/8/2021
[DOB-851] - ED to Hosp-Admission (Discharged) in NH PAV 2 Giles (continued)
Imaging (continued)
CT of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast dated September 6, 2021.
INDICATION: abd pain. Motor vehicle crash. Lower abdominal pain.
TECHNIQUE: Routine IV contrasted CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed. Dose reduction techniques were employed
per ALARA protocol.
COMPARISON: Correlation is made with a CT lung screening exam dated May 14, 2021
FINDINGS: Motion artifact degrades image quality, especially at the lung bases and upper abdomen.
There may be fatty infiltration of the liver. Gallbladder, spleen and adrenal glands are grossly unremarkable given motion. Cystic
lesion at the anterior neck of the pancreas measures 1.3 cm and likely has a thin septation. Multiseptated cystic lesion at
the inferior anterior head of the pancreas measures 2 cm. Pancreatic duct in the medial body, head and neck is upper limits of
normal. No biliary duct dilation.
At least partial duplication of the right renal collecting system. Kidneys are malrotated with the renal hilar regions facing
anteriorly.
Urinary bladder is unremarkable. Uterus remains. Prominent vessels flank the uterus, left greater than right which can be seen
with pelvic congestion syndrome.
Stomach is not well-distended. Appendix is dilated with wall thickening and periappendiceal inflammatory change. Appendix
measures 1 cm in diameter. No bowel obstruction. No free intraperitoneal air. No ascites or evidence of abscess.
Atherosclerotic vascular calcification. No lymphadenopathy.
Sequelae from granulomatous disease within the chest. Left basilar atelectasis/scarring.
There are degenerative changes with no suspicious osseous lesion. No evidence of fracture and for limitations from motion.
IMPRESSION:
1. Acute appendicitis. No free intraperitoneal air or evidence of abscess. This finding was discussed with Santina Arnold in the
ER 9/6/2021 at 11:31 AM.
2. Cystic pancreatic lesions may be serous or mucinous. Comparison with prior imaging is recommended if available and
attention on follow-up is recommended.
3. Atherosclerosis.
Dictated by: Rebecca Feller, M.D.
Images and Report reviewed and interpreted by: Rebecca Feller, M.D.
<PS><Electronically signed by: Rebecca Feller, M.D.>

SURGICAL PATHOLOGY REPORT MOOheen
Louisville, KY 40202
Patient Name: LANG, BRENDA D. Accession #: S21 -31 51 7
DOB: 9/1/1952 (Age: 69) SEX: F MRN:: EP00142214
Location: NH PAV 2 GILES Collected: 9/6/2021
Physician(s): MATTHEW C BOZEMAN MD Received: 9/7/2021
Reported: 9/10/2021
DIAGNOSIS:
APPENDIX, APPENDECTOMY:
APPENDICEAL SERRATED LESIONS, SEE COMMENT.
ACUTE APPENDICITIS AND PERFORATION.
PATHOLOGIST COMMENT:
Section show appendiceal serrated lesions as well as acute appendicitis. There no evidence of appendiceal mucinous
neoplasm or mucin pool seen on sections examined. However, there is perforation of appendix and partially denuded
mucosa at the section with serrated lesion. The marked inflammation and perforation preclude the complete
evaluation. Close clinical follow-up with patient is recommended.
The case was reviewed for QA (MN, TJ, LS).
**Electronically Signed Out on 9/10/2021 **
JIAN SHEN MD
jen/9/8/2021
Interpretation performed at:
NH -Norton Hospital
200 E. Chestnut St
Louisville, KY 40202
CLINICAL HISTORY:
CLINICAL HISTORY AND SPECIAL REQUESTS APPENDICITIS
SPECIMEN SOURCE:
APPENDIX
GROSS DESCRIPTION:
Received in formalin designated "appendix". The specimen consists of a 6.6 cm in length x 0.6 cm in diameter
vermiform appendix with up to 1.7 cm of attached mesoappendix. The serosal surface is pink-gray and slightly
dusky. The proximal stapled margin is inked black. Sectioning reveals pink-red to pink-white hemorrhagic mucosa
anda focal area of perforation present in the mid to distal aspect extending into the surrounding mesoappendiceal
soft tissue. No discrete lesions are appreciated. Representative sections are submitted sequentially from proximal to
distal in cassettes A1-A3 with A1 to include the proximal margin, en face and A3 to include the tip, bisected and
entirely submitted. The remaining mucosa is submitted entirely in cassettes A4-A8.
ds/9/7/2021
MORGAN KELTY
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:
Addiotnal deeper of A7 levels are obtained and reviewed.
Michael R. Nowacki, M.D. - Medical Director Page 1 of 2

DIAGNOSIS:
APPENDIX, APPENDECTOMY:
APPENDICEAL SERRATED LESIONS, SEE COMMENT.
ACUTE APPENDICITIS AND PERFORATION.
PATHOLOGIST COMMENT:
Section show appendiceal serrated lesions as well as acute appendicitis. There
no evidence of appendiceal mucinous neoplasm or mucin pool seen on sections
examined. However, there is perforation of appendix and partially denuded mucosa
at the section with serrated lesion. The marked inflammation and perforation
preclude the complete evaluation. Close clinical follow-up with patient is
recommended.
The case was reviewed for QA (MN, TJ, LS).
**Electronically Signed Out on 9/10/2021 **

Admin instructions: Do not use if serum creatinine is greater than 2, and notify physician. Serum Potassium Level 3.5 To 3.9 mg/dL: Give 20 meq Now & 20 meq in Two
Hours. Repeat Serum Potassium Level 2 Hours After Last Dose. Serum Potassium Level 3.1 To 3.4 mg/dL: Give 40 meq Now & 20 meq in Two
Hours. Repeat Serum Potassium Level 2 Hours After Last Dose. For Serum Potassium Level 3 mg/dL or Less : Give 40 meq Now & 20 meq in
Two Hours. Notify Physician. Repeat Serum Potassium Level 2 Hours After Last Dose and Draw Serum
Magnesium Level. If Magnesium is Less Than 1.6 mg/dL, Then Institute the Magnesium Replacement Protocol. Package: 0245-5319-89

Admin instructions: Do not use if serum creatinine is greater than 2, and notify physician.
Serum Potassium Level 3.5 To 3.9 mg/dL: Give 20 meq Now & 20 meq in Two
Hours. Repeat Serum Potassium Level 2 Hours After Last Dose.
Serum Potassium Level 3.1 To 3.4 mg/dL: Give 40 meq Now & 20 meq in Two
Hours. Repeat Serum Potassium Level 2 Hours After Last Dose.
For Serum Potassium Level 3 mg/dL or Less : Give 40 meq Now & 20 meq in
Two Hours. Notify Physician.
Repeat Serum Potassium Level 2 Hours After Last Dose and Draw Serum
Magnesium Level. If Magnesium is Less Than 1.6 mg/dL,
Then Institute the Magnesium Replacement Protocol.

Order comments: - If serum potassium level is equal to or less than 2.5 mmol/L-or- equal to or greater than 6 mmol/L. - If serum
potassium level remains less than 4 mmol/L after 24 hrs on Potassium Replacement Protocol. - If no baseline serum creatinine on

[DOB-768] - ED to Hosp-Admission (Discharged) in NH PAV 2 Giles (continued)
Care Plan (continued)
Dates: Start: 09/06/21
Description: Use this problem when pressure ulcers are classified as stage I or II. Disciplines: Interdisciplinary
Goal: Skin integrity is maintained or improved
Dates: Start: 09/06/21 Expected End: 09/09/21
Description: Assess and monitor skin integrity. Identify patients at risk for skin breakdown on admission and per
policy. Collaborate with interdisciplinary team and initiate plans and interventions as needed. Disciplines: Interdisciplinary
Goal: Fluid and electrolyte balance are achieved/maintained
Dates: Start: 09/06/21 Expected End: 09/09/21
Description: Assess and monitor vital signs (orthostatic vitals if applicable), fluid intake and output, urine color, labs, 
skin turgor, mucous membranes, jugular venous distention, edema, circumference of edematous
extremities and abdominal girth, respiratory status, and mental status. Monitor for signs and
symptoms of hypovolemia (tachycardia, rapid breathing, decreased urine output, postural hypotension, 
confusion, syncope). Monitor for signs and symptoms of hypervolemia (strong rapid pulse, shortness
of breath, difficulty breathing lying down, crackles heard in lung fields, edema). Collaborate with
interdisciplinary team and initiate plan and interventions as ordered. Disciplines: Interdisciplinary

Description: Assess and monitor vital signs (orthostatic vitals if applicable), fluid intake and output, urine color, labs,

extremities and abdominal girth, respiratory status, and mental status. Monitor for signs and
symptoms of hypovolemia (tachycardia, rapid breathing, decreased urine output, postural hypotension,
confusion, syncope). Monitor for signs and symptoms of hypervolemia (strong rapid pulse, shortness
of breath, difficulty breathing lying down, crackles heard in lung fields, edema). Collaborate with
interdisciplinary team and initiate plan and interventions as ordered.

Problem: Potential for Infection
Dates: Start: 09/06/21
Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary
Goal: Remains infection free
Dates: Start: 09/06/21 Expected End: 09/09/21
Description: Assess and monitor vital signs, skin (color, moisture, integrity, turgor), respiratory status, urinary and
gastrointestinal status, and labs (WBC, cultures). Administer antibiotics and antipyretics as ordered. Ensure aseptic care of all intravenous lines, invasive tubes/drains and wounds. Monitor for signs and
symptoms of infection (redness, warmth, discharge, increased body temperature). Wash hands
properly before and after each patient care activity. Follow isolation guidelines per hospital
protocol/policy. Collaborate with interdisciplinary team and initiate plan and interventions as ordered. Disciplines: Interdisciplinary

Point: Positioning/Pressure Relief (Done)
When in bed, teach that it is important to reposition at least every 2 hours. When in a chair, reposition at least every hour, if they are able to shift themselves, they should do so every 15 minutes. Encourage patient to sit up in chair for all meals unless contraindicated.
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver on how to identify healthy coping mechanisms. Refer to Social Service, Care Management, or Spiritual Care or ChildLife, or Expressive Therapy, if needed. Learning Progress Summary

[DOB-790] - ED to Hosp-Admission (Discharged) in NH PAV 2 Giles (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
Topic: Pain Management (Done)
Point: Skin care (Done)
Use creams or oils on skin to maintain adequate skin moisture content. Avoid cold or dry air. Avoid temperature extremes. Prevent skin breakdown that could lead to bed sores. Have heels float in the air over edge of pillow.
Point: Pain Medication Actions & Side Effects (Done)
Encourage patient to drink plenty of fluids and to eat fruits and vegetables. Educate patient/family/caregiver to take pain medication with food to prevent nausea. Provide medication specific handouts when available.
Point: Discuss the Use of Pain Control Measures Before Pain Becomes Severe (Done)
Point: Explain Pain Rating Scale (Done)
Learning Progress Summary
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver that skin should be inspected at least once daily. Learning Progress Summary
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver that pain medication can cause constipation. Explain that a stool softener might need to be 
given while taking pain medication. Patient Friendly Description: -IV Pain Medication
Your nurse can call your doctor for an order and administer IV pain medication if indicated. This can be given through
your IV and lasts 1-2 hours. This may make you feel sleepy or dizzy. Learning Progress Summary
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver that it is important to always let staff know if having difficulty breathing, pain or discomfort
before pain becomes severe.
Learning Progress Summary
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver on the appropriate pain rating scale. Encourage patient/family/caregiver to monitor patient's 
pain.

[DOB-792] - ED to Hosp-Admission (Discharged) in NH PAV 2 Giles (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
Point: Non-Pharmacological Comfort Measures (Done)
Point: Patient Controlled Analgesia (Done)
Point: Epidural Information (Done)
Caution the patient and other family/visitors that only the patient should press the button for pain relief to decrease the chance
of getting too much pain medicine. Written information available for reinforcement of education.
Patient Friendly Description: -Your nurse will ask you to rate our pain on a scale of zero (0), for no pain, to ten (10), the
worst pain you can experience. -Your nurse will ask you to set a pain goal within zero (0) to ten (10) so that he or she can be sure that what we are doing
is working. -Mild pain is 1-3, Moderate pain is 4-6 and severe pain is 7-10. -We may not be able to take away ALL pain, so try to identify a point that pain would be manageable. -So if your pain goal is 4 or less and you are rating your pain at 7, your nurse would give you a pain medication and then
re-check your pain to ensure it is moving toward your pain goal of 4. Learning Progress Summary
Description:
Explain to patient/family/caregiver that there are other ways of controlling pain than medication. The following are
suggestions: position change, aromatherapy, deep slow breathing, distraction, quiet environment, imagery, heat therapy
and/or cold therapy, laughter, massage, music, physical therapy, and touch therapy. Learning Progress Summary
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver on the use of Patient Controlled Analgesia. Give the patient written information on Patient
Controlled Analgesia. Explain how the pump works. Demonstrate pushing the button to give pain medicine to the patient. Caution the patient and other family/visitors that only the patient should press the PCA button for pain relief to decrease the
chance of getting too much pain medicine. Learning Progress Summary
Description:
Educate the patient/family/caregiver on the use of Epidural Anaglesia. Explain why an epidural is used, how the epidural is
placed and how the medication is given. If the epidural is PCA, demonstrate pushing the button to give pain medicine to the 
patient.
Patient Friendly Description: This is a procedure where an anesthesiologist or Certified Registered Nurse Anesthetist
(CRNA) will place a catheter in your back that provides continuous medication during your labor to keep you comfortable. -The anesthesiologist or CRNA will assess if you can have an epidural and will discuss risks of the procedure with you
prior to placement. -The epidural will make you feel numb from our waist down. It should take away sharp pain, but you will still feel pressure 
during labor. While pressure may be uncomfortable, it helps you know when to start pushing.

Prevent skin breakdown that could lead to bed sores.
Have heels float in the air over edge of pillow.

Patient Friendly Description: -IV Pain Medication
Your nurse can call your doctor for an order and administer IV pain medication if indicated. This can be given through
your IV and lasts 1-2 hours. This may make you feel sleepy or dizzy.

[DOB-795] - ED to Hosp-Admission (Discharged) in NH PAV 2 Giles (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
Patient Friendly Description: -Your nurse will ask you to rate our pain on a scale of zero (0), for no pain, to ten (10), the
worst pain you can experience.
-Your nurse will ask you to set a pain goal within zero (0) to ten (10) so that he or she can be sure that what we are doing
is working.
-Mild pain is 1-3, Moderate pain is 4-6 and severe pain is 7-10.
-We may not be able to take away ALL pain, so try to identify a point that pain would be manageable.
-So if your pain goal is 4 or less and you are rating your pain at 7, your nurse would give you a pain medication and then
re-check your pain to ensure it is moving toward your pain goal of 4.

Point: Non-Pharmacological Comfort Measures (Done)
Description:
Explain to patient/family/caregiver that there are other ways of controlling pain than medication. The following are
suggestions: position change, aromatherapy, deep slow breathing, distraction, quiet environment, imagery, heat therapy
and/or cold therapy, laughter, massage, music, physical therapy, and touch therapy.

Point: Patient Controlled Analgesia (Done)
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver on the use of Patient Controlled Analgesia. Give the patient written information on Patient
Controlled Analgesia. Explain how the pump works. Demonstrate pushing the button to give pain medicine to the patient.
Caution the patient and other family/visitors that only the patient should press the PCA button for pain relief to decrease the
chance of getting too much pain medicine.
Learning Progress Summary
Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by KG at 9/7/2021 1906

Point: Epidural Information (Done)
Description:
Educate the patient/family/caregiver on the use of Epidural Anaglesia. Explain why an epidural is used, how the epidural is
placed and how the medication is given. If the epidural is PCA, demonstrate pushing the button to give pain medicine to the
patient.
Caution the patient and other family/visitors that only the patient should press the button for pain relief to decrease the chance
of getting too much pain medicine. Written information available for reinforcement of education.
Patient Friendly Description: This is a procedure where an anesthesiologist or Certified Registered Nurse Anesthetist
(CRNA) will place a catheter in your back that provides continuous medication during your labor to keep you comfortable.
-The anesthesiologist or CRNA will assess if you can have an epidural and will discuss risks of the procedure with you
prior to placement.
-The epidural will make you feel numb from our waist down. It should take away sharp pain, but you will still feel pressure
during labor. While pressure may be uncomfortable, it helps you know when to start pushing.

Follow all instructions on signs posted in the patient care area. You may be asked to wear gowns, gloves
mask or shoe covers while with the patient. You are asked to wear these items to protect your clothing and
body from possible germs that could be spread from person to person. You should remove these isolation
items before leaving the patient's room and then ALWAYS clean your hands so you do not spread any
germs throughout the hospital.

Patient Education (continued)
Pneumococcal vaccine is strongly recommended by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention for
people with certain medical problems and anyone over 65 years old. This vaccine helps protect against
some bacteria germs that cause pneumonia and make a person very sick. Please talk to the healthcare
team about getting your Pneumococcal Vaccine.
Flu vaccine is strongly recommended by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention for most people to
get every year. Please talk to the healthcare team about getting your Flu Vaccine.
Point: Alcohol Cessation Counseling (Done)
The effects of alcoholism are quite far reaching. Alcohol affects every body system, causing a wide range of health problems. Such problems include poor nutrition, memory disorders, difficulty with balance and walking, liver disease (including cirrhosis
and hepatitis), high blood pressure, weakness of muscles (including the heart), disturbances of heart rhythm, anemia, clotting
disorders, weak immunity to infections, inflammation and irritation along the entire gastrointestinal system, acute and
chronic problems with the pancreas, low blood sugar, high blood fat content, interference with reproductive fertility, and weak
bones.
On a personal level, alcohol can be responsible for marital and other relationship difficulties, depression, unemployment, child
abuse, and general family dysfunction. Alcoholism causes or contributes to a variety of severe social problems: homelessness, murder, suicide, injury, and violent
crime. Alcohol is a contributing factor in 50% of all deaths due to motor vehicle accidents. In fact, more than 100,000 deaths
occur each year due to the effects of alcohol, of which 50% are due to injuries of some sort. In the United States, the annual
economic cost of alcoholism and alcohol abuse is estimated at more than $160 billion. Assess patients for alcohol-related problems and initiate discussion of such problems with not only the patient, but also the
family, significant others, and appropriate members of an interdisciplinary health team. Point: Influenza Vaccine (Done)
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver on Pneumococcal vaccine. If patient has not had Pneumococcal vaccine (a vaccine that
protects from one of the most serious forms of pneumonia), have patient check with their doctor to see if they should receive 
this. Instruct the patient to ask the doctor about the influenza (flu) vaccine each year in the fall. Patient Friendly Description:

Topic: Disease Management (Done)
Point: Smoking Cessation Counseling (Done)
Counsel patient on smoking cessation and smoking cessation programs offered in the community. Clinician counseling should include: Practical suggestions for quitting, managing withdrawal symptoms, and getting support. Explain effects smoking and second hand smoke have on the body. Encourage the patient to ask people that smoke around him/her to smoke outside or in another room. Refer patient to Cardiopulmonary Rehabilitation, if applicable. During counseling, use motivational interviewing techniques, including: * Open-ended questions (EX: "What are some of the reasons you would like to quit smoking?") * Affirming statements (EX: "It’s great that you are motivated to quit smoking") * Reflective listening (EX: "It sounds like trying to quit smoking has been frustrating for you")
Point: Limit Visitors for Rest (Done)
Point: Managing Disease & Disease Process (Done)
Point: Disease Complications (Done)
Point: Importance of Following Medical Treatment as Prescribed (Done)

Educate patient/family/caregiver on smoking cessation. Provide smoking cessation booklet.

The effects of alcoholism are quite far reaching. Alcohol affects every body system, causing a wide range of health problems.
Such problems include poor nutrition, memory disorders, difficulty with balance and walking, liver disease (including cirrhosis
and hepatitis), high blood pressure, weakness of muscles (including the heart), disturbances of heart rhythm, anemia, clotting
disorders, weak immunity to infections, inflammation and irritation along the entire gastrointestinal system, acute and

On a personal level, alcohol can be responsible for marital and other relationship difficulties, depression, unemployment, child
abuse, and general family dysfunction.
Alcoholism causes or contributes to a variety of severe social problems: homelessness, murder, suicide, injury, and violent
crime. Alcohol is a contributing factor in 50% of all deaths due to motor vehicle accidents. In fact, more than 100,000 deaths
occur each year due to the effects of alcohol, of which 50% are due to injuries of some sort. In the United States, the annual

Topic: Health and Safety (Done)
Patients or patient's guardians have a right to voice safety concerns related to care and environment. Please talk to the unit staff and a nurse leader. If you still have concerns, you may dial 0 and ask to speak
to the administrator on call.
You should let the healthcare team know if you are concerned with any changes in the patient's condition. If
you remain concerned and do not think everything is being done – you have the option to call the Rapid
Response Team. A team of trained staff will come to the patient's bedside to check on them. DIAL 3333
and tell them you need the Rapid Response Team. Tell them the patient's name and room number.
Germs are spread when you cough or sneeze. Please try to “cover your cough.” Cover your mouth and
nose with a tissue and throw away the tissue. If you do not have a tissue, cover your mouth and nose with
the bend or crook of your arm at your elbow. Wash your hands often.

[DOB-805] - ED to Hosp-Admission (Discharged) in NH PAV 2 Giles (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
Point: Smoking Cessation Counseling (Done)
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver on smoking cessation. Provide smoking cessation booklet.
Counsel patient on smoking cessation and smoking cessation programs offered in the community.
Clinician counseling should include: Practical suggestions for quitting, managing withdrawal symptoms, and getting support.
Explain effects smoking and second hand smoke have on the body.
Encourage the patient to ask people that smoke around him/her to smoke outside or in another room.
Refer patient to Cardiopulmonary Rehabilitation, if applicable.
During counseling, use motivational interviewing techniques, including:
*
Open-ended questions (EX: "What are some of the reasons you would like to quit smoking?") *
Affirming statements (EX: "It's great that you are motivated to quit smoking")
*
Reflective listening (EX: "It sounds like trying to quit smoking has been frustrating for you")

Patient Education (continued)
Clean your hands often with soap and clean water or use an alcohol based hand sanitizer to help prevent
the spread of germs. Always clean your hands before and after doing any care, making food or eating food, and using the bathroom.
The hospital environment is different from your home. Help us keep you and your loved ones safe from falls. 
Ask for help before getting out of bed. Ask how many side rails should stay up at all times. Wear footwear
that will not slip on the floors. Keep the room free from clutter. Some patients may use a bed alarm for their
safety that will alert staff if the patient is trying to get out of bed.
Point: Activity (Done)

Have them demonstrate proper hand washing technique using soap, water, friction, clean nails under running water, and dry
hands on clean towel without touching dirty surfaces and using alcohol based decontaminates.

* Instruct to wear non-slip footwear when out of bed

* Communicate "at risk" status with patient and family
* If discharging home, instruct on home modifications to reduce falls in the home

[DOB-816] - ED to Hosp-Admission (Discharged) in NH PAV 2 Giles (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
Topic: Discharge Planning (Done)
You and your care team will work together to make sure you are ready to go home when you are getting
better. Please be planning who can pick you up from the hospital so finding transportation does not delay
you going home. Many things need to be completed before you can go home. It may take several hours
once you know you are going home until the time you are ready to leave the hospital. Use this time to make
sure you have all the information and know how to do all the medical care needed at home. Also begin
gathering all items you need to take back home.
Point: Community Resources (Done)

will be provided, review the role of the home care nurse and
when to expect the first visit. Provide written instructions as indicated. Explain to family that they should prepare the home environment for safety/falls prevention if indicated (minimize trip hazards, keep areas well lit, avoid slippery, wet floors, etc). Use caution near pets (pets can be a tripping hazard).
If the patient has an incision instruct them to not allow pets to get near the incision so as to decrease the chance of infection.

Topic: Anticoagulant Therapy (Done)
Point: Blood Transfusion (Done)
In a blood transfusion, donated blood is added to patient's own blood. A blood transfusion may also be done to supplement
various components of the patient's blood with donated blood products. During a typical blood transfusion, certain parts of blood are delivered through an intravenous (IV) line that's placed in the
patient. A blood transfusion usually takes two to four hours, though in an emergency it can be done much faster. A blood transfusion boosts blood levels that are low, either because the body isn't making enough or because blood has been
lost during surgery, injury or disease

Point: Anticoagulant Diet (Done)
Green leafy vegetables are high sources of vitamin K. Spinach, kale and greens such as turnip, collard and mustard provide
very high sources of the vitamin. Other good sources of vitamin K include broccoli, asparagus, Brussels sprouts, scallions and
lettuce. Salad dressings and oils such as olive, soybean and canola also contain vitamin K. Point: Medications - AnticoagulantTherapy (Done)
any monitoring that will be done to evaluate the medication and verify its effectiveness; significant side effects including drug- drug, drug-food interactions or therapeutic contraindications that may be encountered and how to avoid and respond to such
factors; action to be taken in the event or a wrong or missed dose; next dose due. Give medication specific teaching material. Explain the action of medication, reason for taking, side effects, signs of allergic reaction, and when the physician should be
called. Reinforce that this medication should be taken exactly as the physician has prescribed. The physician will order blood tests to see how long the blood takes to clot while taking this medication. The dose of this medication may be changed according to the results of these tests. Explain the importance of follow-up monitoring. Thrombolytics are medications given to quickly dissolve the blood clot.

[DOB-824] - ED to Hosp-Admission (Discharged) in NH PAV 2 Giles (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
These medications are delivered through a catheter that is directed into the area of the clot. They are used to treat large clots causing severe symptoms. Because they increase the risk of bleeding, they are only used in special situations determined by your physician. These
situations may include massive swelling of an arm or leg or in situations where a blood clot in the lungs (pulmonary embolism)
has 
left the patient very short of breath or with low blood pressure. Point: Educate & Provide Patient with Written Information on Adverse Reactions - Anticoagulant (Done)
Explain the side effects, signs of allergic reaction, and when the physician should be called. Reinforce that this medication
should be taken exactly as the physician has prescribed. Explain that all anticoagulant treatment increases the risk of bleeding. Warfarin causes fetal abnormalities - chrondrodysplasia 
punctata, and asplenia - and may increase fetal loss rate; skin necrosis and rarer adverse reactions. Unfractionated heparins
can cause osteoporosis, thrombocytopenia with or without thrombosis, and other rare reactions. Low molecular weight
heparins are less likely to do so. Point: Educate & Provide Patient with Written Information on Drug Interactions - Anticoagulant (Done)
Many commonly used medications interact with warfarin, as do some foods (particularly fresh plant-based foods containing
vitamin K) and its activity has to be monitored by blood testing for the international normalized ratio (INR) to ensure an
adequate yet safe dose is taken. Many commonly used antibiotics, such as metronidazole or the macrolides, will greatly increase the effect of warfarin by
reducing the metabolism of warfarin in the body. Other broad-spectrum antibiotics can reduce the amount of the normal
bacterial flora in the bowel, which make significant quantities of vitamin K, thus potentiating the effect of warfarin. Common medications that must be discussed with your doctor before taking if ordered in combination with warfarin: * Advair Diskus (fluticasone / salmeterol) * Aspirin Low Strength (aspirin) * Calcium 600 D (calcium / vitamin d) * Crestor (rosuvastatin) * Cymbalta (duloxetine) * Fish Oil (omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids) * Lantus (insulin glargine) * Lasix (furosemide) * Lipitor (atorvastatin) * Lyrica (pregabalin) * Metoprolol Succinate ER (metoprolol) * Metoprolol Tartrate (metoprolol) * Nexium (esomeprazole) * Plavix (clopidogrel) * Spiriva (tiotropium) * Synthroid (levothyroxine) * Tylenol (acetaminophen)
Learning Progress Summary
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver on the potential for adverse drug reactions of the Anticoagulant prescribed.
Learning Progress Summary
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver on the potential for Interactions of the Anticoagulant prescribed.

Point: Anticoagulant Diet (Done)
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver that there is no special diet for individuals who are prescribed anticoagulants. However,
health care providers should inform patients of certain nutrients in foods that interact with their medication.
Green leafy vegetables are high sources of vitamin K. Spinach, kale and greens such as turnip, collard and mustard provide
very high sources of the vitamin. Other good sources of vitamin K include broccoli, asparagus, Brussels sprouts, scallions and
lettuce. Salad dressings and oils such as olive, soybean and canola also contain vitamin K.
Learning Progress Summary

Point: Medications - AnticoagulantTherapy (Done)
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver on name, reason, dose, route, and frequency for taking the Anticoagulant prescribed;
special instructions and precautions for preparing, self-administering, using, or disposing the medications in the hospital or
home;
any monitoring that will be done to evaluate the medication and verify its effectiveness; significant side effects including drugdrug, drug-food interactions or therapeutic contraindications that may be encountered and how to avoid and respond to such
factors; action to be taken in the event or a wrong or missed dose; next dose due. Give medication specific teaching material.
Explain the action of medication, reason for taking, side effects, signs of allergic reaction, and when the physician should be
called. Reinforce that this medication should be taken exactly as the physician has prescribed.
The physician will order blood tests to see how long the blood takes to clot while taking this medication.
The dose of this medication may be changed according to the results of these tests.
Explain the importance of follow-up monitoring.
Thrombolytics are medications given to quickly dissolve the blood clot.

Point: Educate & Provide Patient with Written Information on INR Monitoring - Anticoagulant (Done)
Dosing of warfarin is complicated by the fact that it is known to interact with many commonly used medications and even with
chemicals that may be present in certain foods. These interactions may enhance or reduce warfarin's anticoagulation effect.
In order to optimize the therapeutic effect without risking dangerous side effects such as bleeding, close monitoring of the
degree of anticoagulation is required by blood testing (INR). During the initial stage of treatment, checking may be required
daily; intervals between tests can be lengthened if the patient manages stable therapeutic INR levels on an unchanged
warfarin dose. Warfarin interacts with many commonly used drugs, and the metabolism of warfarin varies greatly between patients. Some
foods have also been reported to interact with warfarin. Apart from the metabolic interactions, highly protein bound drugs can
displace warfarin from serum albumin and cause an increase in the INR. This makes finding the correct dosage difficult, and
accentuates the need of monitoring; when initiating a medication that is known to interact with warfarin (e.g. simvastatin), INR
checks are increased or dosages adjusted until a new ideal dosage is found. Excessive use of alcohol is also known to affect the metabolism of warfarin and can elevate the INR. Patients are often
cautioned against the excessive use of alcohol while taking warfarin. Point: Educate & Provide Patient with Written Information on Vitamin K Dietary Management - Anticoagulant (Done)
Foods containing large quantities of vitamin K will reduce the warfarin effect. The liver uses vitamin K to make blood clotting
proteins. In doing so, vitamin K plays a role in the body's natural clotting process. Warfarin works against vitamin K. Specifically, warfarin reduces the liver's ability to use vitamin K to produce normally functioning forms of the blood clotting
proteins. By reducing the liver's ability to use vitamin K to produce normally functioning forms of the blood clotting proteins, warfarin reduces the risk of forming a blood clot. A significant change in vitamin K intake can result in a significant, and potentially dangerous, change in the INR.
It is a common misconception that people on warfarin should avoid vitamin K. Rather than avoiding vitamin K, the patient
should maintain a consistent intake of vitamin K by maintaining a consistent diet. In order to maintain a consistent intake of
vitamin K, they need to know something about the vitamin K content of the foods they eat. As a "rule of thumb", green
vegetables, especially leafy green vegetables, and certain oils have a high content of vitamin K. Most fruits, meats, dairy
products, and grains are low in vitamin K.
Learning Progress Summary
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver that a high INR predisposes to a high risk of bleeding, while an INR below the therapeutic
target indicates that the dose of warfarin is insufficient to protect against thromboembolic events.
Learning Progress Summary
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver that Warfarin interacts with some foods (particularly fresh plant-based foods containing
vitamin K) and its activity has to be monitored by blood testing for the international normalized ratio (INR) to ensure an
adequate yet safe dose is taken.

Patient Education (continued)
These medications are delivered through a catheter that is directed into the area of the clot.
They are used to treat large clots causing severe symptoms.
Because they increase the risk of bleeding, they are only used in special situations determined by your physician. These
situations may include massive swelling of an arm or leg or in situations where a blood clot in the lungs (pulmonary embolism)
has
left the patient very short of breath or with low blood pressure.

Point: Educate & Provide Patient with Written Information on Adverse Reactions - Anticoagulant (Done)
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver on the potential for adverse drug reactions of the Anticoagulant prescribed.
Explain the side effects, signs of allergic reaction, and when the physician should be called. Reinforce that this medication
should be taken exactly as the physician has prescribed.
Explain that all anticoagulant treatment increases the risk of bleeding. Warfarin causes fetal abnormalities - chrondrodysplasia
punctata, and asplenia - and may increase fetal loss rate; skin necrosis and rarer adverse reactions. Unfractionated heparins
can cause osteoporosis, thrombocytopenia with or without thrombosis, and other rare reactions. Low molecular weight
heparins are less likely to do so.
Learning Progress Summary
Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by KG at 9/7/2021 1906
Acceptance, Explanation, Teach Back by KP at 9/6/2021 1449
Point: Educate & Provide Patient with Written Information on Drug Interactions - Anticoagulant (Done)
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver on the potential for Interactions of the Anticoagulant prescribed.
Many commonly used medications interact with warfarin, as do some foods (particularly fresh plant-based foods containing
vitamin K) and its activity has to be monitored by blood testing for the international normalized ratio (INR) to ensure an
adequate yet safe dose is taken.
Many commonly used antibiotics, such as metronidazole or the macrolides, will greatly increase the effect of warfarin by
reducing the metabolism of warfarin in the body. Other broad-spectrum antibiotics can reduce the amount of the normal
bacterial flora in the bowel, which make significant quantities of vitamin K, thus potentiating the effect of warfarin.
Common medications that must be discussed with your doctor before taking if ordered in combination with warfarin: *
Advair Diskus (fluticasone / salmeterol)
* Aspirin Low Strength (aspirin)
* Calcium 600 D (calcium / vitamin d)
*
Crestor (rosuvastatin)
*
Cymbalta (duloxetine) *
Fish Oil (omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids) *
Lantus (insulin glargine)
* Lasix (furosemide)
* Lipitor (atorvastatin)
* Lyrica (pregabalin)
* Metoprolol Succinate ER (metoprolol)
*
Metoprolol Tartrate (metoprolol)
* Nexium (esomeprazole) *
Plavix (clopidogrel) *
Spiriva (tiotropium)
*
Synthroid (levothyroxine)
* Tylenol (acetaminophen)

Doing any of the following may also influence the amount of vitamin K in the body: * Increasing vegetable intake as part of a diet to lose weight * Eating more fresh vegetables in spring and summer because of the larger selection available
* Being sick and unable to eat solid foods for a few days
* Returning to normal diet after being hospitalized and on a limited diet due to surgery or illness
* Changing diet due to traveling to a place where the foods are unfamiliar * Deciding to change diet to eat more lentils, beans, dried peas, and vegetables
***** If the diet or eating patterns is changed for any reason it is very important that the physician be notified.

Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver that a high INR predisposes to a high risk of bleeding, while an INR below the therapeutic
target indicates that the dose of warfarin is insufficient to protect against thromboembolic events.
Dosing of warfarin is complicated by the fact that it is known to interact with many commonly used medications and even with
chemicals that may be present in certain foods. These interactions may enhance or reduce warfarin's anticoagulation effect.
In order to optimize the therapeutic effect without risking dangerous side effects such as bleeding, close monitoring of the
degree of anticoagulation is required by blood testing (INR). During the initial stage of treatment, checking may be required
daily; intervals between tests can be lengthened if the patient manages stable therapeutic INR levels on an unchanged
warfarin dose.
Warfarin interacts with many commonly used drugs, and the metabolism of warfarin varies greatly between patients. Some
foods have also been reported to interact with warfarin. Apart from the metabolic interactions, highly protein bound drugs can
displace warfarin from serum albumin and cause an increase in the INR. This makes finding the correct dosage difficult, and
accentuates the need of monitoring; when initiating a medication that is known to interact with warfarin (e.g. simvastatin), INR
checks are increased or dosages adjusted until a new ideal dosage is found.

Topic: Treatments/Procedures - Diabetes (Done)
A microbiology/immunology test is a test done to find what kind of germs that may be spreading through the blood or
body. Germs from infected parts of your body can travel into your blood. The test can provide the medical team with
information to help take care of the patient.
A chemistry and electrolyte test is a test done to check to make sure a person has the correct amount of
minerals and electrolytes like sugar, salts, potassium, calcium and many other electrolytes in their blood or
body. It is very important for good health to have the correct amount of these electrolytes in the body. This
test can provide the medical team with information to help take care of the patient.

A urine test is a test that can give the medical team some information about what is in your urine (pee) and
about the kidneys and how well they are working. This test can provide the medical team with information to
help take care of the patient.
A blood gas is a test that can give the medical team some information about the lungs, heart and some about the
kidneys and how well they are working. It can measure oxygen and carbon dioxide levels in the blood. It also
measures the acid or base level in the blood which is called the PH of the blood. This test can provide the medical
team with information to help take care of the patient.

Patient Friendly Description:
A urine test is a test that can give the medical team some information about what is in your urine (pee) and
about the kidneys and how well they are working. This test can provide the medical team with information to
help take care of the patient.

Point: Blood Gases (Done)
Description:
Educate the patient/family/caregiver on blood gas tests ordered to include: what the test is, why it is being done, preparation
for the test, how the test will be performed, and what to expect during and after the test.
Patient Friendly Description:
A blood gas is a test that can give the medical team some information about the lungs, heart and some about the
kidneys and how well they are working. It can measure oxygen and carbon dioxide levels in the blood. It also
measures the acid or base level in the blood which is called the PH of the blood. This test can provide the medical
team with information to help take care of the patient.

Point: When to Call the Doctor (Done)
Description:
Instruct the patient/family/caregiver to call their health care provider if: 1. there are symptoms of hypo or hyperglycemia not
controlled by simple measures, 2. skin on the foot becomes red, itchy, swollen or is painful, 3. feet are persistently cold,
4. corns or calluses occur despite preventive measures, 5. cramps occur in the legs or feet.

Topic: Disease Management - Diabetes (Done)
Some medical problems may require medical treatments and follow up medical care for long periods of time
or even for life. You may hear this called a chronic disease. It is very important to know what care is needed
and to follow the instructions from the medical team to help you stay well. Keep all follow-up appointments
and ask questions to stay informed about your medical needs.

Instruct the patient/family/caregiver on the use and care of the insulin pump. Demonstrate proper programming on insulin
pump settings based on physician's orders. Have patient/caregiver return demonstrate care of the insertion site, changing
the
cassette, changing the
insulin reservoir and programming the insulin pump. Allow the patient/caregiver to practice giving various types of boluses.
Review basal/bolus therapy and benefits of using insulin pump. Instruct the patient/caregiver on proper infusion site insertion
and rotation. Allow the patient to insert first infusion site. Refer to Diabetic Nurse Educator if
applicable.

Point: Treatment of Hypoglycemia (Done)
Explain that when blood sugar levels are too low, eating something with carbohydrates can help. Encourage them to keep high-carbohydrate snacks on hand. Blood glucose levels can be raised quickly by eating or drinking something that contain 15 grams of glucose or carbohydrates
containing glucose. The following are a few good options: * 4 to 6 ounces of fruit juice
* 3 to 4 glucose tablets
* 3 to 5 peppermint hard candies
* 8 to 10 small flavored Life Savers candies
* 4 to 6 ounces of a regular soft drink
Treatment if mild to moderate hypoglycemia: treat blood glucose level of less than 70 (or otherwise directed by healthcare
provider) with 15 grams of rapid acting carbohydrate. Recheck blood glucose in 15 minutes. If needed, treat with additional
15 grams of rapid acting carbohydrate. Treatment of severe hypoglycemia: Use glucagon emergency kit. Point: Prevention of Hypoglycemia (Done)
management and learning to detect and treat hypoglycemia early before it
gets worse.

Topic: Diabetic Ketoacidosis Management (Done)
A neurovascular check is a way to quickly assess if a person is having any weakness or changes in the way
their muscles, nerves and parts of the body are working. The healthcare provider will touch on different
areas of the body and ask the person to do specific movements with their arms and legs. If the provider
finds any signs that something is wrong, then other test can be done. These neurovascular checks can
provide the medical team with information to help take care of the patient.

Point: Treatment of Hypoglycemia (Done)
Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver on the treatment for hypoglycemia. Include teaching the use of glucagon. Have them
return demonstrate giving glucagon in a simulated environment.
Explain that when blood sugar levels are too low, eating something with carbohydrates can help.
Encourage them to keep high-carbohydrate snacks on hand.
Blood glucose levels can be raised quickly by eating or drinking something that contain 15 grams of glucose or carbohydrates
containing glucose. The following are a few good options:
* 4 to 6 ounces of fruit juice
* 3 to 4 glucose tablets
* 3 to 5 peppermint hard candies
* 8 to 10 small flavored Life Savers candies
* 4 to 6 ounces of a regular soft drink
Treatment if mild to moderate hypoglycemia: treat blood glucose level of less than 70 (or otherwise directed by healthcare
provider) with 15 grams of rapid acting carbohydrate. Recheck blood glucose in 15 minutes. If needed, treat with additional
15 grams of rapid acting carbohydrate.
Treatment of severe hypoglycemia: Use glucagon emergency kit.

Patient Education (continued)
NPO means absolutely nothing to eat or drink. When a person is scheduled for certain medical procedures
or for any surgery, they MUST be NPO (or have nothing to eat or drink) for a certain amount of time. This is
for the safety of the patient. Also, sometimes when a person is sick it is best for them to have nothing to eat
or drink by mouth for a certain amount of time. Please let your care team know when was the last time the
patient had anything to eat or drink.
Some illnesses or diseases can lead to electrolyte imbalances. You get most of the needed electrolytes from
the foods you eat and drink. Difficulty eating, drinking, or taking in enough nutrition can cause this. Any
problems that cause lots of vomiting, diarrhea or losing body fluids can cause this. You may need to be
given extra electrolytes to help correct the problem. Signs and symptoms of electrolyte imbalance may
include: • Fast, slow or irregular heart rate
• Dizzy or lightheaded, feeling like you may faint • Sweating • Confusion
• Very, very tired
When a person is sick and may need to be in the hospital, it is common for them to need fluids giving to
them through an I.V or intravenous line. When you are sick, you may not be able to get enough liquids or
