Medication Information for After Discharge
How to transfer refills on a prescription:
¢ If you have refills on a prescription and would like to fill them at a different pharmacy: take your bottle(s) with the
prescription information to the pharmacy where you wish to have them filled. Or you can call the pharmacy where
you wish to fill them and notify them you would like to transfer your prescription(s). You will need to provide them
with information located on the bottle.
* To assure you do not miss doses, notify the pharmacy 2 to 3 days before you will run out.
How to get refills on medications that were started in the hospital:
¢ Itis common for doctors that discharge you from the hospital to only provide you with a short duration of
medication(s).
¢ This does not mean that you do not need to stay on the medication(s), but they want you to be seen by your
primary doctor to assess how you are doing and determine if you should stay on the same medication(s).
¢ It is very important that you have an appointment with your primary doctor within 30 days of discharge. At your
appointment, be sure to take any new medications with you and ask them to provide you with new prescriptions if
they feel you need to stay on them.
Safe medication storage and disposal
Medications should be stored by patients in secure locations and excess pills should be disposed of properly, to minimize
the risk of diversion and inappropriate use. To find a prescription drug disposal location near you please visit https://
odcp.ky.gov
If Prescribed Narcotic Medications:
Your providers may have already given you your prescription for pain medication prior to your discharge date. If this is the
case, it is best to be prepared and have your medication filled and ready for your return home from the hospital. You may
want to have your pain pills with you on your ride home if you are traveling a long distance.

Medication Information for After Discharge (continued)
While at Home:
¢ Use medication only as directed.
« Remember medication takes time to work. Most oral pain relievers need at least 20 minutes to take effect.
¢ Remember to take your pain medication before activity and at bedtime. Your provider may advise you to take your
pain medication at regular intervals (such as every 4 to 6 hours).
¢ Be sure to get enough rest. If you are having trouble sleeping, talk to your provider.
¢ Use pillows to support you when you sleep and when you do your coughing and deep breathing exercises
* Try using the alternative methods such as heating pads or cold therapy, walking, guided imagery tapes, listening
to soft music, changing your position in bed, aromatherapy, and massage can help relieve your pain.
Third Party Apps and Epic
Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record for
their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.
For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.
Your Same Day Care Options
We're committed to providing same-day care options, close to home, with the ease of online scheduling. Receive care with
a Norton Healthcare provider in person at one of our primary care, Norton Immediate Care or Norton Prompt Care at
Walgreens locations, or schedule a virtual visit with Norton eCare from the comfort of home. Access all of your care
options at NortonNow.com.
Norton Care Call
If you are going home after leaving the hospital you will receive a Norton Care Call
¢ Within 48 hours of discharge, you should receive an automated phone call from [PHONE-882] (Caller ID will read
Norton Care Call)
e You will be asked a few questions about your health and progress. Please answer the questions using the phone
keypad.
¢ Based on your answers, a Norton Healthcare team member may call you to offer help and other instructions.

 CONDITIONS OF ADMISSION AND 
AUTHORIZATION FOR TREATMENT □ Norton Children's Hospital □ Norton Hospital □ Norton Audubon Hospital □ Norton Women’s and Children’s Hospital □ Norton Brownsboro Hospital □ Norton Children’s Medical Center
I. CONSENT TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTS, PROCEDURES, AND MEDICAL TREATMENT: 
I voluntarily consent to care that involves routine diagnostic tests, procedures, and medical treatment as prescribed by my physician and performed by the facility. No guarantee has been given by 
anyone as to the results that may be obtained. This consent includes testing for Hepatitis, Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) or any other blood-borne infectious diseases, if ordered for diagnostic 
purposes or due to an occupational exposure. I consent to photographing, videotaping, video monitoring and audio devices for medical, scientific, safety or education purposes as well as for the 
purposes of identification. I understand that these will be stored in a secure manner that will protect my privacy. Images that identify me will only be released when authorized by me or as needed to 
provide for my continued medical treatment or identification.
II. OTHER PRACTITIONERS AND SUPPLIERS OF SERVICES:
I understand and acknowledge that I may require the services of physicians, groups of physicians or other practitioners (such as nurses and physician assistants) who are not employees of the facility,
including, but not limited to, anesthesiologists, radiologists, emergency department physicians, certified registered nurse anesthetists, and other practitioners. I also understand that residents, fellows,
medical students, and students of various health care fields may be involved in my care and that they are not employees of the facility. I agree that the signing of this document does not in any way
imply that Norton Healthcare is responsible for or assumes any liability for the activities of any such physicians or practitioners who are not its employees. When a physician, other practitioner or supplier of services bills me directly, I agree to pay the bill. I agree that the provisions regarding the release of information and assignment of benefits set out in paragraphs III and V below shall apply
not only to the facility but also to such physicians, practitioners and suppliers of services and their claims for payment.
III. RELEASE OF INFORMATION:
I authorize the facility to notify my referring and treating physician(s), of my admission to the facility and to release any information about me as requested by such physician(s). I authorize the facility
and any other holder of medical or other information about me including medical information concerning drug related conditions, alcoholism, psychological conditions, psychiatric conditions, and/or
blood-borne infectious diseases, to release to the Social Security Administration, its intermediaries and its carriers, State agencies, the review organization employed by my employer, or the employer of
the insured member of my family, or any private third-party payor, as appropriate, any information required for the completion of any claim for benefits arising out of services rendered to me either on
an inpatient or outpatient basis. I further authorize the release of my social security number to the manufacturer of any implanted medical device in accordance with the Medical Device Tracking Act of
the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). I authorize the release of my HIV test results to healthcare personnel in the event of an occupational exposure. If my injury is work-related, I authorize the
facility and any other holder of medical or other information about me to release from my medical record any information as requested by the company and/or its workers compensation administrator as
indicated below:

I understand that the facility maintains a safe for the safekeeping of money and valuables and the facility shall not be liable for the loss of or damage to any money, jewelry, documents, or any other
items of personal property, unless deposited in the facility safe for safekeeping. Furthermore, I accept full responsibility for items of personal property kept in my possession.
V. ASSIGNMENT OF BENEFITS:
I agree to the assignment of all third-party benefits to the facility and agree to pay the facility for all charges not covered by third-party payors. In the case of outpatient services I agree that this
document shall remain in full force and effect until specifically revoked by me in writing. Any sums not paid by a third-party payor are my obligation.
VI. RELEASE OF INSURANCE INFORMATION TO FACILITY:
I agree to the release by my insurance carrier to the facility and the facility's billing service of any eligibility and utilization data concerning my insurance coverage which the facility and/or its billing 
supplier may require. I authorize the facility to file an appeal of a payment denial and/or request a hearing regarding any denial of payment for treatment provided to me. I further authorize the facility 
to present proof, utilize facility records and my records and documentation for appeals and hearings.
VII. PAYMENT OF FACILITY BILLS WHEN DUE:
I am liable for the payment of the account of the facility and I am obligated to pay the account of the facility, in accordance with the regular rates and terms of the facility. If my injury is work-related, I am further obligated to pay the account of the facility in the event my employer or its third-party administrator denies payment for workers compensation. It is understood that the uninsured portion of
bills are payable at the end of each week and in full upon discharge from the facility. I understand that after reasonable notice, delinquent accounts may be turned over to a collection agency and/or an
attorney for collection. Should it be necessary to place this account in the hands of an attorney for collection, I agree to pay the costs of collection, including any court costs and a reasonable fee to the
attorney employed by the facility to collect the account.
I agree that the facility, its employees, agents, attorneys and collection agencies, may call the cell number I provide for any reason, including but not limited to inquiring about the status and collection of 
my account. I also expressly authorize that each of them may use any mechanism, including but not limited to automatic dialing, artificial and prerecorded voice systems.
VIII.HIV/AIDS INFORMATION: I acknowledge receipt of an HIV/AIDS informational brochure.
IX. SMOKING CESSATION COUNSELING (18 YEARS AND OLDER): I acknowledge receipt of smoking cessation counseling informational handout.
X. PRIVACY NOTICE: I acknowledge that a copy of Norton's Notice of Privacy Practices has been made available to me.
XI. PATIENT BILL OF RIGHTS: I acknowledge that a copy of the Patient Bill of Rights has been made available to me.
The undersigned agrees that a copy of this release and assignment may be used in place of the original copy. The undersigned certifies that he/she has read and agrees to this agreement and has received 
a copy. The undersigned certifies that the patient is unable to consent or is a minor and the undersigned hereby consents to the above as indicated below.

Important Message from Medicare
Your Rights as a Hospital Inpatient:
You can receive Medicare covered services. This includes medically necessary hospital services and services
you may need after you are discharged, ifordered by your doctor. You have a right to know about these
services, who will pay for them, and where you can get them.
You can beinvolved in any decisions about your hospital stay.
You can report any concerns you have about the quality of care you receive to your QIO at: KEPRO 1-813-280-
8256. The QIO is the independent reviewer authorized by Medicare to review the decision to discharge you.
You can work with the hospital to prepare for your safe discharge and arrange for services you may need after
you leave the hospital. When you no longer need inpatient hospital care, your doctor or the hospital staff will
inform you ofyour planned discharge date.
You can speak with your doctor or other hospital staff ifyou have concerns about being discharged.
Your Right toAppeal Your Hospital Discharge:
You have the right to an immediate, independent medical review (appeal) of the decision to discharge you
from the hospital. If you dothis, you will nothave topay for the services you receiveduring the appeal
(except for charges like copays and deductibles).
If you choose to appeal, the independent reviewer will ask for your opinion. The reviewer also will look at
your medical records and/or other relevant information. You donot have to prepare anything in writing, but
you have the right to do so ifyou wish.
If you choose to appeal, you and the reviewer will each receive a copy ofa detailed explanation about why
your covered hospital stay should not continue. You will receive this detailed notice only after you request an
appeal.
Ifthe QIO finds that you are not ready to bedischarged from the hospital, Medicare will continue to cover
your hospital services.
Ifthe QIO agrees services should no longer becovered after the discharge date, neither Medicare nor your
Medicare health plan will pay for your hospital stay after noon of the day after the QIO notifies you of its

Important Message from Medicare
Your Rights as a Hospital Inpatient:
You can receive Medicare covered services. This includes medically necessary hospital services and services
you may need after you are discharged, if ordered by your doctor. You have a right to know about these
services, who will pay for them, and where you can get them.
You can be involved in any decisions about your hospital stay.
You can report any concerns you have about the quality of care you receive to your QIO at: KEPRO 1-813-280-
8256. The QIO is the independent reviewer authorized by Medicare to review the decision to discharge you.
You can work with the hospital to prepare for your safe discharge and arrange for services you may need after
you leave the hospital. When you no longer need inpatient hospital care, your doctor or the hospital staff will
inform you of your planned discharge date.
You can speak with your doctor or other hospital staff if you have concerns about being discharged.
Your Right to Appeal Your Hospital Discharge:
You have the right to an immediate, independent medical review (appeal) of the decision to discharge you
from the hospital. If you do this, you will not have to pay for the services you receive during the appeal
(except for charges like copays and deductibles).
If you choose to appeal, the independent reviewer will ask for your opinion. The reviewer also will look at
your medical records and/or other relevant information. You do not have to prepare anything in writing, but
you have the right to do so if you wish.
If you choose to appeal, you and the reviewer will each receive a copy of a detailed explanation about why
your covered hospital stay should not continue. You will receive this detailed notice only after you request an
appeal.
If the QIO finds that you are not ready to be discharged from the hospital, Medicare will continue to cover
your hospital services.
If the QIO agrees services should no longer be covered after the discharge date, neither Medicare nor your
Medicare health plan will pay for your hospital stay after noon of the day after the QIO notifies you of its

PATIENT INFORMATION
TRANSFUSION OF BLOOD, BLOOD COMPONENTS & RH IMMUNE GLOBULIN
Consent: Your doctor may determine that a blood transfusion is needed as part ofyour medical care. Itis important that you or your legal
representative understand the risks and benefits of receiving a blood component transfusion. Your doctor will explain (or has explained) to
you or your legal representative the risks and benefits of a blood component transfusion and any alternative treatment(s) to a blood
transfusion. The following information on blood and blood components is being given to you or your legal representative to assist you or
your legal representative in making an informed decision about receiving a blood transfusion. Before you or your legal representative sign
the informed consent to receive a blood transfusion, your doctor or medical professional will be available to answer any questions you
may have about blood and blood transfusions. Please ask these individuals to explain anything that you did not understand about the
risks and benefits of the blood transfusion you may require.
Benefits: Tests performed during your hospitalization may show that you need blood. Blood is made of many elements. Which elements
you should get and the amount to be given are decisions made only by your doctor.
* Red Blood cells - carry oxygen to your tissue and organs
* Granulocytes - white cells needed to fight infection
* Platelets - help clot your blood
* Cryoprecipitate -Several proteins needed to clot your blood
* Plasma -_ liquid portion assists in clotting your blood
Testing the blood you receive: Volunteer donors, that undergo strict screening, donate blood. Many tests (in addition to the blood type)
are performed on this blood and are tested by the blood supplier:
* Syphilis (a sexually transmitted disease)
* Hepatitis B and C (viruses that injure the liver)
* HIV (viruses that cause AIDS)
* HTLV (human T-cell lymphotrophic viruses which may cause blood and nerve
disease)
* WNV West Nile Virus (virus that may cause Encephalitis)
Any blood donation found to be positive for any of these tests is destroyed. Finally, if you are going to receive red blood cells, the hospital
blood bank will test a sample of your blood and that of the acceptable donated blood unit to make sure that the blood is compatible
(crossmatching your blood).
Risks: Blood should be thought of as a drug having certain risks or side effects. Some patients may have a mild reaction to a blood
transfusion such as fever, chills, hives or body aches. More severe reactions such as breathing difficulties and shock occur very rarely.
Human error, although uncommon, may also occur during preparation ofyour blood component for transfusion.
The risk of getting an infectious disease such as hepatitis or HIV from.a blood transfusion is very rare.
infectious Agent Estimated Risk (per unit) As compared with the risks with voluntary or involuntary activities:
° Hepatitis B virus 1:280,000 Activity Risks (death/person/year)
« Hepatitis C virus 1:1,149,000 « Smoking 1:200
° Other (malaria, chagas, babesiosis) 1:1,000,000 e Automobile Driving 1;10,000
« HIV 1:1,467,000 ¢ Tornado 1:455,000
« HTLV 1:641,000-1,900,000 « Influenza 1:5,000
e West Nile Virus 1:7,000,000
Alternative Treatments:
*Autologous blood transfusion refers to procedures in which you may serve as your own blood donor. In some cases, this may be done
prior to your hospitalization. Autologous units are reserved for your use and kept until the expiration date if not used by you. They cannot
ever be given to:-another person if not used by you. If this blood is insufficient for your transfusion needs, then blood components from
other donors will be used.
* In other.cases, blood lost during surgery can be washed and given back to you, (called intraoperative cell salvage or cell saver). Those
with religious objections can opt fora cell saver with a continuous closed circuit.
* In-other situations, you may be able to select the donors. of your blood (family or friends) and receive a directed donation. Studies have
shown, however, that these donations are no safer than that from the community blood supply. Directed units that you .do not use will be
released on the day of your hospital discharge for use by other patients unless your physician notifies the blood bank to hold those units
for your use in the future or until the expiration date.
* Bloodless medicine/surgery techniques (called Blood Conservation) may be appropriate for selected patients in certain circumstances.

PATIENT INFORMATION
TRANSFUSION OF BLOOD, BLOOD COMPONENTS & RH IMMUNE GLOBULIN
Consent: Your doctor may determine that a blood transfusion is needed as part of your medical care. It is important that you or your legal
representative understand the risks and benefits of receiving a blood component transfusion. Your doctor will explain (or has explained) to
you or your legal representative the risks and benefits of a blood component transfusion and any alternative treatment(s) to a blood
transfusion. The following information on blood and blood components is being given to you or your legal representative to assist you or
your legal representative in making an informed decision about receiving a blood transfusion. Before you or your legal representative sign
the informed consent to receive a blood transfusion, your doctor or medical professional will be available to answer any questions you
may have about blood and blood transfusions. Please ask these individuals to explain anything that you did not understand about the
risks and benefits of the blood transfusion you may require.
Benefits: Tests performed during your hospitalization may show that you need blood. Blood is made of many elements. Which elements
you should get and the amount to be given are decisions made only by your doctor.
Red Blood cells carry oxygen to your tissue and organs
* Granulocytes white cells needed to fight infection
Platelets help clot your blood
Cryoprecipitate -Several proteins needed to clot your blood
Plasma liquid portion assists in clotting your blood
Testing the blood you receive: Volunteer donors, that undergo strict screening, donate blood. Many tests (in addition to the blood type)
are performed on this blood and are tested by the blood supplier:
*
Syphilis (a sexually transmitted disease)
Hepatitis B and C (viruses that injure the liver)
HIV (viruses that cause AIDS)
HTLV (human T-cell lymphotrophic viruses which may cause blood and nerve
disease)
WNV West Nile Virus (virus that may cause Encephalitis)
Any blood donation found to be positive for any of these tests is destroyed. Finally, if you are going to receive red blood cells, the hospital
blood bank will test a sample of your blood and that of the acceptable donated blood unit to make sure that the blood is compatible
(crossmatching your blood).
Risks: Blood should be thought of as a drug having certain risks or side effects. Some patients may have a mild reaction to a blood
transfusion such as fever, chills, hives or body aches. More severe reactions such as breathing difficulties and shock occur very rarely.
Human error, although uncommon, may also occur during preparation of your blood component for transfusion.
The risk of getting an infectious disease such as hepatitis or HIV from a blood transfusion is very rare.
Infectious Agent Estimated Risk (per unit) As compared with the risks with voluntary or involuntary activities:
Hepatitis B virus 1:280,000 Activity Risks (death/person/year)
Hepatitis C virus 1:1,149,000 Smoking 1:200
Other (malaria, chagas, babesiosis) 1:1,000,000 Automobile Driving 1;10,000
HIV 1:1,467,000 Tornado 1:455,000
HTLV 1:641,000-1,900,000 Influenza 1:5,000
West Nile Virus 1:7,000,000
Alternative Treatments:
* Autologous blood transfusion refers to procedures in which you may serve as your own blood donor. In some cases, this may be done
prior to your hospitalization. Autologous units are reserved for your use and kept until the expiration date if not used by you. They cannot
ever be given to another person if not used by you. If this blood is insufficient for your transfusion needs, then blood components from
other donors will be used.
* In other cases, blood lost during surgery can be washed and given back to you, (called intraoperative cell salvage or cell saver). Those
with religious objections can opt for a cell saver with a continuous closed circuit.
* In other situations, you may be able to select the donors of your blood (family or friends) and receive a directed donation. Studies have
shown, however, that these donations are no safer than that from the community blood supply. Directed units that you do not use will be
released on the day of your hospital discharge for use by other patients unless your physician notifies the blood bank to hold those units
for your use in the future or until the expiration date.
* Bloodless medicine/surgery techniques (called Blood Conservation) may be appropriate for selected patients in certain circumstances.

HISTORY: Postmenopausal screening. FINDINGS: In postmenopausal females and males above the age of 50, T-score is the preferred method of evaluating bone
mineral density. In these patients, the WHO classification defines osteoporosis for T-scores less than -2.5, osteopenia between
-1.0
and -2.5, and normal bone mineral density greater than -1.0. For the L1-L4, bone mineral density measures 0.978 g/cm^2. T-score measures -1.6. Z-score measures 0.8. For the total left hip, bone mineral density measures 0.852 g/cm^2. T-score measures -1.1. Z-score measures 0. For the left femoral neck, bone mineral density measures 0.846 g/cm^2. T-score measures -0.7. Z-score measures 0.7. Based on FRAX score, the ten year probability of a major osteoporotic fracture is 3%, and the ten year probability of hip fracture
is <1%.
The lowest T-score occurs at the lumbar spine measuring -1.6.
IMPRESSION:
1. Osteopenia diagnosed at the lumbar spine and total left hip. 2. Fracture risk is increased above normal. Dictated by: Terry Williams, M.D.
Images and Report reviewed and interpreted by: Terry Williams, M.D. <PS><Electronically signed by: Terry Williams, M.D.>

HISTORY: Postmenopausal screening.
FINDINGS: In postmenopausal females and males above the age of 50, T-score is the preferred method of evaluating bone
mineral density. In these patients, the WHO classification defines osteoporosis for T-scores less than -2.5, osteopenia between
-1.0
and -2.5, and normal bone mineral density greater than -1.0.
For the L1-L4, bone mineral density measures 0.978 g/cm^2. T-score measures -1.6. Z-score measures 0.8.
For the total left hip, bone mineral density measures 0.852 g/cm^2. T-score measures -1.1. Z-score measures 0.
For the left femoral neck, bone mineral density measures 0.846 g/cm^2. T-score measures -0.7. Z-score measures 0.7.
Based on FRAX score, the ten year probability of a major osteoporotic fracture is 3%, and the ten year probability of hip fracture
is <1%.
The lowest T-score occurs at the lumbar spine measuring -1.6.
IMPRESSION:
1. Osteopenia diagnosed at the lumbar spine and total left hip.
2. Fracture risk is increased above normal.

I. CONSENT TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTS, PROCEDURES, AND MEDICAL TREATMENT:
I voluntarily consent to care that involves routine diagnostic tests, procedures, and medical treatment as prescribed by my physician and performed by the
facility. No guarantee has been given by anyone as to theresults that may be obtained. This consent includes testing for Hepatitis, Human
Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) or any other blood-borne infectious diseases, ifordered for diagnostic purposes or due to an occupational exposure. I
consent to photographing, videotaping, video monitoring and audio devices for medical, scientific, safety or education purposes as well as for the purposes
of identification. I understand that these will be stored in asecure manner that will protect my privacy. Images that identify me will only be released when
authorized by me or as needed toprovide for my continued medical treatment or identification.
II. OTHER PRACTITIONERS AND SUPPLIERS OF SERVICES:
I understand and acknowledge that I may require the services of physicians, groups of physicians or other practitioners (such as nurses and physician
assistants) who are not employees of the facility, including, but not limited to, anesthesiologists, radiologists, emergency department physicians, certified
registered nurse anesthetists, and other practitioners. I also understand that residents, fellows, medical students, and students of various health care fields
may be involved in my care and that they are not employees of the facility. I agree that the signing of this document does not in any way imply that
Norton Healthcare is responsible for or assumes any liability for the activities of any such physicians or practitioners who are not its employees. When a
physician, other practitioner or supplier of services bills me directly, I agree to pay the bill. I agree that the provisions regarding the release of
information and assignment of benefits set out in paragraphs III, V, and VI below shall apply not only to the facility but also to such physicians,
practitioners and suppliers of services and their claims for payment.
Ill. RELEASE OF INFORMATION:
I authorize the facility to notify my referring andtreating physician(s), of my admission to the facility and to release any information about me as
requested by such physician(s). I authorize the facility andany other holder of medical or other information about me including medical information
concerning drug related conditions, alcoholism, psychological conditions, psychiatric conditions, and/or blood-borne infectious diseases, to release to the
Social Security Administration, its intermediaries and its carriers, State agencies, the review organization employed bymy employer, or the employer of
theinsured member of my family, or any private third-party payor, as appropriate, any information required for thecompletion of any claim for benefits
arising out of services rendered to me either on an inpatient or outpatient basis. I further authorize the release of my social security number to the
manufacturer of any implanted medical device in accordance with the Medical Device Tracking Act of the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). I
authorize therelease of my HIV test results to healthcare personnel in theevent of an occupational exposure. If my injury is work-related, I authorize the
facility and any other holder of medical or other information about me to release from my medical record any information as requested by the company

V.ASSIGNMENT OF BENEFITS:
I agree to the assignment of all third-party benefits to the facility and agree to pay the facility for all charges not covered by third-party payors. In the
case of outpatient services I agree that this document shall remain in full force and effect until specifically revoked by me in writing. Any sums not paid
by a third-party payor are my obligation.

VII. PAYMENT OF FACILITY BILLS WHEN DUE:
I am liable for thepayment of the account of the facility and I am obligated to pay the account of the facility, in accordance with the regular rates and

administrator denies payment for workers compensation. It is understood that theuninsured portion of bills are payable atthe end of each week and in full
upon discharge from the facility. I understand that after reasonable notice, delinquent accounts may be turned over to a collection agency and/or an
attorney for collection. Should it benecessary to place this account in the hands of an attorney for collection, I agree to pay the costs of collection,
including any court costs and areasonable fee to the attorney employed by the facility to collect the account.
I agree that the facility, its employees, agents, attorneys and collection agencies, may call or text the cell number I provide for any reason, including but
not limited to inquiring about the status and collection of my account. I also expressly authorize that each of them may use any mechanism, including but
not limited to automatic dialing, artificial and prerecorded voice systems.

I. CONSENT TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTS, PROCEDURES, AND MEDICAL TREATMENT:
I voluntarily consent to care that involves routine diagnostic tests, procedures, and medical treatment as prescribed by my physician and performed by the
facility. No guarantee has been given by anyone as to the results that may be obtained. This consent includes testing for Hepatitis, Human
Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) or any other blood-borne infectious diseases, if ordered for diagnostic purposes or due to an occupational exposure. I
consent to photographing, videotaping, video monitoring and audio devices for medical, scientific, safety or education purposes as well as for the purposes
of identification. I understand that these will be stored in a secure manner that will protect my privacy. Images that identify me will only be released when
authorized by me or as needed to provide for my continued medical treatment or identification.
II. OTHER PRACTITIONERS AND SUPPLIERS OF SERVICES:
I understand and acknowledge that I may require the services of physicians, groups of physicians or other practitioners (such as nurses and physician
assistants) who are not employees of the facility, including, but not limited to, anesthesiologists, radiologists, emergency department physicians, certified
registered nurse anesthetists, and other practitioners. I also understand that residents, fellows, medical students, and students of various health care fields
may be involved in my care and that they are not employees of the facility. I agree that the signing of this document does not in any way imply that
Norton Healthcare is responsible for or assumes any liability for the activities of any such physicians or practitioners who are not its employees. When a
physician, other practitioner or supplier of services bills me directly, I agree to pay the bill. I agree that the provisions regarding the release of
information and assignment of benefits set out in paragraphs III, V, and VI below shall apply not only to the facility but also to such physicians,
practitioners and suppliers of services and their claims for payment.
III. RELEASE OF INFORMATION:
I authorize the facility to notify my referring and treating physician(s), of my admission to the facility and to release any information about me as
requested by such physician(s). I authorize the facility and any other holder of medical or other information about me including medical information
concerning drug related conditions, alcoholism, psychological conditions, psychiatric conditions, and/or blood-borne infectious diseases, to release to the
Social Security Administration, its intermediaries and its carriers, State agencies, the review organization employed by my employer, or the employer of
the insured member of my family, or any private third-party payor, as appropriate, any information required for the completion of any claim for benefits
arising out of services rendered to me either on an inpatient or outpatient basis. I further authorize the release of my social security number to the
manufacturer of any implanted medical device in accordance with the Medical Device Tracking Act of the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). I
authorize the release of my HIV test results to healthcare personnel in the event of an occupational exposure. If my injury is work-related, I authorize the
facility and any other holder of medical or other information about me to release from my medical record any information as requested by the company
and/or its workers compensation administrator as indicated below:

The following views were performed: Bilateral craniocaudal; bilateral craniocaudal with tomosynthesis; bilateral mediolateral
oblique; and bilateral mediolateral oblique with tomosynthesis. Images were reviewed with digital R2 Computer-Aided Detection 
(CAD).
There are scattered areas of fibroglandular density. There are no suspicious masses, significant calcifications, or other abnormalities seen. Tomosynthesis was utilized for this examination.
IMPRESSION:
There is no mammographic evidence of malignancy. Screening Mammogram in 1 year is recommended. BI-RADS Category 1:
Negative

I voluntarily consent to care that involves routine diagnostic tests, procedures, and medical treatment as prescribed by my physician and performed by the
facility. No guarantee has been given by anyone as to the results that may be obtained. This consent includes testing for Hepatitis, Human
Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) or any other blood-borne infectious diseases, if ordered for diagnostic purposes or due to an occupational exposure. I
consent to photographing, videotaping, video monitoring and audio devices for medical, scientific, safety or education purposes as well as for the purposes
of identification. I understand that these will be stored in a secure manner that will protect my privacy. Images that identify me will only be released when
authorized by me or as needed to provide for my continued medical treatment or identification.
II. OTHER PRACTITIONERS AND SUPPLIERS OF SERVICES:
I understand and acknowledge that I may require the services of physicians, groups of physicians or other practitioners (such as nurses and physician
assistants) who are not employees of the facility, including, but not limited to, anesthesiologists, radiologists, emergency department physicians, certified
registered nurse anesthetists, and other practitioners. I also understand that residents, fellows, medical students, and students of various health care fields
may be involved in my care and that they are not employees of the facility. I agree that the signing of this document does not in any way imply that
Norton Healthcare is responsible for or assumes any liability for the activities of any such physicians or practitioners who are not its employees. When a
physician, other practitioner or supplier of services bills me directly, I agree to pay the bill. I agree that the provisions regarding the release of information
and assignment of benefits set out in paragraphs III, V, and VI below shall apply not only to the facility but also to such physicians, practitioners and suppliers of services and their claims for payment.
III. RELEASE OF INFORMATION:
